title: Flourishing in Copperplate — ornamental extensions
subtitle: Adding hairline loops and swash strokes to Copperplate capitals and ascenders
excerpt: Copperplate flourishes are the ornamental hairline extensions that surround the capitals and ascenders in formal penmanship. This tutorial covers the underlying oval logic of flourishes, the safe places to add them, and the strokes most commonly used.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), chapter on ornamental penmanship and flourishing. Edward Cocker, The Pen's Triumph (1658), ornamental alphabets and flourish demonstrations. The British Library's digitised penmanship copybooks (pre-1928) are the reference for the flourish vocabulary here.

glossaryTerms.definition: An ornamental hairline extension to a letter's stroke, typically forming loops, spirals, or long sweeping curves. In Copperplate, flourishes are always hairlines; shade on a flourish is an error.
A capital letter with an extended ornamental stroke, usually a hairline loop that begins well above the cap height and sweeps around the letter.

content.text: is always a hairline. In Copperplate penmanship, pressure on a flourish is an error: the ornament has no shade of its own; the shade belongs to the letter it decorates. The classical

in the Spencer copybooks demonstrate the approach: the letter is complete, and the flourish is a separate movement added before or after the letter is finished, never interrupting the letter's own shade.

items.name: Hunt 101 nib (very flexible, needed for fine hairlines on large loops)
Iron-gall ink, well diluted (slightly thinner than normal writing consistency for hairlines)
Hot-press watercolour paper or plate-finish bristol board
Tracing paper (for overlaying classical exemplars from the Spencerian copybooks)

content.text: Every Copperplate flourish is either an oval, a partial oval, or a sequence of nested ovals. The same 52 to 55-degree slope that governs the letter forms also governs the flourishes. Before attempting any specific flourish, practise drawing large, slow, even oval spirals that spiral outward: start small at the centre, spiral out in hairlines only, returning to the entry point. The spirals should be even, at the correct slope, and all hairline. If any part of the spiral shades, the pressure is wrong.
Types of flourish and where they go

The most common flourish on a Copperplate capital is the entry loop that begins above the letter: a large oval hairline starting 2 to 3 cap-heights above the writing line, sweeping down and to the right to enter the capital at its normal entry point. For H, the loop begins far above and to the left, sweeps down and to the right in a broad oval, and arrives at the top of the H's stem. For L, the loop begins above and sweeps into the large curved top of the letter.
Exit loops on capitals and final letters

The last letter in a word or the last word on a line can carry an exit loop: a hairline that continues past the letter's normal exit point, sweeps in a wide loop below the baseline, and rises back to cross the word or extend to the right margin. The most useful exit loop is on the final s, which naturally ends with a small tail that can be extended into a long sweeping flourish below the text.

In formal Spencerian pieces, the ascenders of h, k, l, and b often carry a small loop at the top, formed by the hairline entry curling back on itself before the shade downstroke. Similarly, the descenders of g, y, j, p, and z carry a looped return rather than a simple exit hairline. These ascender and descender loops are integral to the letter forms in the ornate style; the basic Copperplate taught in the minuscule tutorial omits them, but they can be added once the basic letterforms are consistent.

A flourish must not cross itself within the same letter's stroke space. A flourish must not crowd the letters adjacent to it. Flourishes belong on the first letter of a piece, the last letter, and isolated important capitals in the middle. Flourishing every letter makes the text unreadable. The test: cover the flourishes and check the text reads clearly; then reveal the flourishes and check they add rather than obscure.

attrs.heading: Common errors when adding flourishes
attrs.intro: Problems that appear in the first attempts at ornamental extension.

items.symptom: Flourishes add shade where they cross themselves
items.cause: Pressure not fully released on the hairline strokes, particularly when the hand changes direction.
items.fix: Practise direction changes on hairlines only. The pen must have zero downward pressure on any flourish stroke.

items.symptom: Flourishes look different on every letter
items.cause: Each flourish improvised in the moment rather than designed before writing.
items.fix: Plan the flourish path on tracing paper first. Trace it several times until the shape is memorised, then write the full letter with its flourish in one movement.
